Fern Por-rrrcAI- Pnecrrces C oMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA 9-5914-2319
te16) 322-5660 o Fax t9l6) 322-0886
September 8, 2008
Chris Raymer
Taxpayers for Maldonado
P.O. Box 948
San Luis Obispo, CA 93406-0948
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4-08-137
Dear Mr. Raymer:
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Taxpayers for
Maldonado regardin-g the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the ..Act,,)l
and is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act
as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Ogleshlt (1g75) I FppC Ops. 71.)
QUESTION
A candidate for elective state office was defeated in the primary election and is
required by Section 853 18 to return contributions for the general election ro contributors
on a pro rata basis. What are the candidate's duties under the Act when a contributor
refuses to accept the returned contribution?
CONCLUSION
If a contriblltor to the general election refuses to accept his or her share of the
returned contributions under Section 85318, the committee shall pay the funds to the
General Fund of the state.
FACTS
You are the treasttrer for Taxpayers for Maldonado. In 2006, Senator Abel
Maldonado ran Llnsllccessfr-rlly for the California State Controller. Senator Maldonado
| 'fhe Political Retirrm Act is contained in Government Code Secti()ns 8l(x)0 through 910l+. ,\ll

codeofRegulations.,\ll regulatoryret'erencesaretol'itle2.Divisioi6oltheCalitirrniaCode,f
Regulatitlns. unless othelwise indicated.


File No. A-08-137
Page No.2
did not make it past the primary election. Prior to the primary election, the campaign had
collected contributions attribLltable to the general election. Because the campaign aia not
make it to the general election, contributions were returned to contributors with
explanation letters after the primary election.
The majority of the contributors deposited their retumed contributions in a timely
manner. However, some contributors have yet to deposit their retuned contributions.
Following llP on these contributors, the campaign ha.s sent a letter explaining the need to
accept the ftrnds and has placed no fewer than two telephone calls to each. Despite the
campaign's efforts, there are still contributors who have not accepted the returned
contributions. Currently, the campaign is waiting to close the campaign bank accollnt
and committee but is being held up by the contributors that have not complied with the
committee's request to accept the returned funds.
AI\ALYSIS
Section 85318 provides the following
"A candidate for elective state office may raise contributions for a general
election prior to the primary election ... for the same elective state office
if the candidate sets aside these contributions and uses these contributions
for the general election . . .. If the candidate for elective state office is
defeated in the primary election . . ., the general election . . . funds shall be
reftrnded to the contributors on a pro rata basis less any expenses
associated with the raising and administration of general election . . .
contributions."
From the facts presented, Taxpayers for Maldonado has been unsuccessful in
returning contributions for the general election to some contributors because the
contributors have refused to accept the returned contributions. While Section 8531g
mandates a "pro rata" retum of contributions accepted for a general election upon the
defeat of a candidate in a primary election, it does not provide a clear mle in those
circumstances in which a contributor refuses to accept his or her proportionate share of
the campaign funds. Accordingly, we must now determine the permissible uses for these
refused general election funds considering the statutory intent of Section 8531g and the
statutory framework of the Act.2
I Wenotethattherearenopreciserulesfbrdeterminingthesufficiencyofacommittee'seflbrtto
return tunds under Section 85318, but a committee is required tcl make a reasonable and good-faith ef'fort.
Under the tacts you have presented. the committee has sent a letter explaining the need t,io.."pt the tirnds
and has placed no f'ewer than two telephone calls to each of the contriLutors r.trting to acceptihe tirnds.
Accordingly. it appears that the committee has made a reasonable and -{tlod-faith etfbrt. However. the
Commission does not ac[ as a flnder of fact in its advice giving capacity. anO the ultimate responsibility tbr
lne af1tt11re of all pertinent f'acts rests with the public otficial. t-See. i.g.. Otlertrrun Advice Letrer. No. A-
02-34O: O'Horra r\dvice Lerter. No. A-00- 174. )


File No. A-08-137
Page No.3
Under the Act, an election is defined as "any primary, general, special or recall
election held in this state." (Section 82022.) Contributions to candidates for elective
state office for any parlicular election are limited by Sections 85301-8530g of rhe Act.
For purposes of the Act's contribution limits, a primary election and a general election
are considered different elections. (Section 82022.)
Section 85318 permits raising general election campaign funds during the primary
election. However, the candidate may use these funds during ihe primary ele-ctiononly 
-
for expenditures associated with the raising and administration of generai election
contributions. Upon the defeat of the candidate, Section 85318 requires the candidate to
return the funds (less any expenses associated with raising and administration of general
election contributions) to contributors towards the general election on a pro rata bisis.
While strict, the requirements of Section 85318 serve the purpose of preventing
candidates defeated in a primary election from circumventing the Aci's contribltion
limits by using general election funds raised during the primary election, which would
otherwise exceed the primary election's applicable contriblrtion li-it., for purposes
unrelated to the general election.l
Accordingly, we mllst conclude that general election funds may not be used for
any purposes other than those enumerated in Section 85318 upon the defeat of the
candidate in the primary election. In your case, where a contributor of the funds has
refused to accept the returned contribution as required, the candidate shall pay the funds
to the General Fund of the state.a
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
C
By: Bnan G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
t This letter is limited to the facts presented. We do not offer any opinion on the legality or
appropriateness ofa contributor refusing to accept general election tunds iefunded by a candldate det'eated
in a primary election, nor do we address whether thls refusal constitutes a violation nf th. A.t', applicable
contribution limits.
4 In comparisol. Section 85701 requires candidates that receive laundered tunds to pay the funds
to the General Fund of the state because allowing a candidate to use laundered funds would be inconsistent
with the purposes of the Act and allow circumvention of the Act's applicable contribution limits. As with
the use clf laundered funds, allowing a candidate to use general electlon funds during the primary election
or upon his or her def'eat in the primary election. except as permitted in Section g53lg. would be
inconsistent with the purposes of the Act and circumvent the Act's applicable contribution limits.
Accordingly. the option most consistent with the Act. if the contribuirir retirses to accept the refund of the
tunds as required by Section tl53l8. is to pay the funds to the General Fund of the state.
